1
0  Extremum-seeking control (ESC) is a real-time and model-
.
free optimization approach which is employed to determine
6
0 and maintain the optimum (extremum) point of the output map
4 of a static or dynamic system with nonlinear and unknown
2 dynamics [23]. For that purpose, the perturbation method [13]
:
v is often employed in the ESC.
i
ESC iteratively or adaptively adjusts the control-input vari-
X
ables in order to decrease or increase the value of a cost or
r
a objective function. This is done so that  the system’s output
is gradually moved towards  the optimal point, which can
be a maximum or minimum of this objective function. ESC
is particularly useful when it  is not possible to obtain an
accurate model of the system or when the system is subject to
disturbances, uncertainties, or unpredictable variations [23].
Literature Review: The ESC idea was initially proposed
by Leblanc  [14]  in  the historical  context  of maximizing
the power transfer  to a tram car. Decades after, Krstic´ and
Wang in [13] have proposed a systematic analysis based on
averaging and singular perturbation theories to prove stability
P. H. S. Coutinho and T. R. Oliveira are with the Department of Electronics
and Telecommunication Engineering,  State University of Rio de Janeiro
(UERJ), Brazil (e-mails: phcoutinho@eng.uerj.br; tiagoroux@uerj.br).
M. Krstic´ is with the Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineer-
ing, University of California - San Diego, USA (e-mail: krstic@ucsd.edu).
Corresponding author: Pedro H. S. Coutinho.
This work was supported by the Brazilian agencies CNPq (Grant numbers:
407885/2023-4 and 309008/2022-0), CAPES and FAPERJ.

1
ol for Scalar Maps with
ffusion PDEs
Roux Oliveira, and Miroslav Krstic´.
for general nonlinear dynamic systems described by ordinary
differential equations (ODEs). Since then, ESC have been
improved in both practical and theoretical frameworks [1]. To
name a few examples, reference [15] addresses the stochastic
versions of ESC, the authors in [8] propose the Newton-based
ESC in order to obtain multiparameter generalization, while
publication [24] employs ESC for  stabilization. Moreover,
ESC has been considered in several applications,  such as
brake system control, autonomous vehicles and mobile robots,
bio-processes optimization,  renewable energy systems,  and
refrigeration system optimization, see  [3],  [11],  [16],  [17],
[26] and references  therein. However,  all  this  literature  is
limited to systems with ﬁnite-dimensional actuation dynamics.
It  is known that actuation dynamics modeled by inﬁnite-
dimensional dynamics can be found in several applications.
For instance, actuator delays in which the delay propagation is
modeled by a ﬁrst-order hyperbolic partial differential equation
(PDE). Another example of an inﬁnite-dimensional system is
the diffusion process that is found in biological, chemical, and
temperature regulation [7], [18], [22].
Within the context of ESC to inﬁnite-dimensional actuation
dynamics, reference [21] addresses ESC with arbitrarily large
delays whose effect is modeled by hyperbolic transport PDEs.
This approach has paved the way for further generalizations to
other classes of PDEs [20]. The authors in [6] handle the ESC
with actuation dynamics governed by diffusion PDEs. More
recently,  the Newton-based extremum seeking extension for
multi-input maps subject to actuation dynamics governed by
distinct PDEs is performed in [19], whereas publication [25]
considers the ESC for a class of wave PDEs with Kelvin-
Voigt damping. However, the aforementioned results on ESC
for PDEs are limited to PDE boundary compensation. Those
results can not be directly applied when distributed actuation
is concerned. The sole ESC publication considering distributed
delays is the paper [27]. Hence, the main goal of this paper
is to expand the ESC framework for dealing with actuation
dynamics governed by distributed diffusion PDEs.
Contributions: Motivated by  the aforementioned dis-
cussion,  this paper deals with the gradient ESC for static
scalar maps with distributed actuation dynamics governed
by diffusion PDEs.  In contrast  to related works  [6]  and
[19], the boundary PDE compensation can not be applied in
the distributed case. Then, inspired by the backstepping-like
method proposed by [4],  the ﬁrst contribution of this paper
is  to develop a compensation controller for  the distributed
diffusion PDE to guarantee the local exponential stability of
the equilibrium of  the closed-loop average error dynamics,
based on Lyapunov stability arguments. Moreover, it is known

fusion PDEs is presented in Section II, including a motivating
example and the deﬁnition of  the signals and system con-
sidered here. The main results are presented in Section III,
where the distributed diffusion PDE compensation controller
is designed. Moreover, we provide the stability analysis via
Lyapunov-based arguments and averaging theory to ensure the
convergence of the original system into a small neighborhood
of the extremum point. Numerical results are presented in Sec-
tion IV. Finally, concluding remarks are drawn in Section V.
Notation: The partial derivatives of a function u(x, t) are
denoted as ∂xu(x, t) = ∂u(x, t)/∂x, ∂tu(x, t) = ∂u(x, t)/∂t.
Whenever convenient, we use the compact form ux(x, t) e
ut(x, t), or simply ux and ut, respectively. The subscript “av”
is used to denote the average of a periodic variable with period
Π. The Euclidean norm of a ﬁnite-dimension state variable
X(t)  is denoted as X(t) . The spatial norm in L2[0, L]

II. PROBLEM FORMULATION
A. A motivating example
Consider a diffusion (heat) PDE given by

This example may represent  the temperature control  in a
building system [2], where the heat ﬂow from a temperature
source θ(t) enters  the  room from the supply ventilation.
Although the temperature gradient near  the ceiling α(x, t)
varies in time, it also varies along the space variable x. Thus,
the heat/cold ﬂow is diffused from the supply to the return vent
α(0, t). Moreover, we can deﬁne the distributed temperature
along the space variable x as follows

Θ(t) =  α(y, t)dy.  (3)

Thus, if we consider an optimum operating point along the
space variable as Θ∗, the control problem can be formulated
by designing a controller such that the variable Θ(t) converges
towards a small neighborhood of the desired average temper-
ature Θ∗. This example illustrates one potential application of
ESC for distributed diffusion PDEs, which consists of driving
the variable Θ(t) towards Θ∗.
B. The standard gradient ESC without PDEs
Consider the standard gradient ESC without PDEs in Fig. 2.
The aim is to optimize an unknown static map y =Q(Θ) by
solving a real-time optimization with unknown output y∗ and
unknown optimizer Θ∗, with measured output y(t)  R and

C. Gradient ESC with Distributed Diffusion PDEs

Fig. 2,  this paper concerns the case in which the actuation
dynamics are governed by a distributed diffusion PDE. With-
out  loss of generality, we consider  the diffusion coefﬁcient
as ǫ = 1.

The main results of this paper are presented in this section.
In Section III-A, we design the controller to compensate for
the distributed diffusion PDE.  In Section III-B,  the average
version of the designed compensation controller is introduced,
from which a control law that can be implemented in real-time
is obtained. Finally, in Section III-C, the stability proof of the
closed-loop system with the modiﬁed compensation controller
is derived.

B. Controller Design for the Average System
Although the controller in (36) has been effectively designed
to compensate for  the distributed diffusion PDE dynamics,
this controller can not be directly implemented in real-time
because it depends on ϑ, which is not available for measure-
ment. To deal with this issue, we adopt the result  in [8] to
the ESC system with distributed diffusion PDE.

With this
extension, we develop a real-time implementable version of
the controller (36).
